description.description: What this pattern is, the UX problem it solves, and how its components compose to meet a user need. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata: the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json).

relationships.description: Links from this pattern to other entities — mainly 'composes' the components it's built from (mark `required` true for the essential ones), plus 'depends-on' and 'alternative-to'. Tools derive the reverse edges. Use `links` metadata for external resources instead.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this pattern, in order. Accepts `interactions`, the shared structural kinds (anatomy, variants, states), and every general kind. Order matters — `interactions` uses it to show the flow over time, and tools SHOULD keep it. Declare the components a pattern uses as `composes` edges in `relationships` (with `role` describing what each does), not as a block.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Docs for agents (AI/LLM) only, using the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Put anything a human needs in `documentBlocks` — that's the default. Use this only for things that would be noise to a person: hard must/must-not rules, notes that correct a mistake agents commonly make (like picking a similar but wrong entity), evidence-backed constraints, and machine-checkable criteria. A rule humans need too still belongs in `documentBlocks`. This adds to the human docs, it never replaces them. Tools MUST NOT show these blocks to humans. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human docs first.

tokenOverride.description: One token override in a theme: which token changes, and why. The value itself lives in the DTCG file — this just records the intent.

token.description: The token being overridden. MUST match a token's `identifier` defined elsewhere in the system. Token identifiers have no fixed pattern, to support DTCG and design-tool naming styles (dots, dashes, slashes).

description.description: What changes for this token in this theme, and why (ex: 'Lightened to meet WCAG AAA contrast on the dark background'). The resolved value lives in the DTCG file.

theme.description: A named context that adapts the system's token values — a color mode (light, dark, high-contrast), a density (compact, comfortable), a brand variant, or a product-specific tweak. DSDS captures the theme's purpose, docs, accessibility notes, and which tokens it affects; the override values live in the DTCG file `source` points to. The token catalog is still the source of truth for token identity, type, and docs.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a theme.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the theme (ex: 'dark', 'high-contrast', 'compact', 'brand-sub-brand').

name.description: Display name for the theme (ex: 'Dark Mode', 'High Contrast', 'Compact Density').

description.description: What this theme is for, the context it adapts the system to, and when to apply it. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata: the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json).

source.description: Points to the DTCG (W3C Design Tokens) file. DSDS doesn't copy token values — the DTCG file is the source of truth for resolved values, aliases, and type.

file.description: URI or relative path to the DTCG file containing this theme's token override definitions.

path.description: The dot-path to this theme's group within the DTCG file, if applicable.

overrides.description: The token overrides this theme applies. List only tokens that differ from the default — anything unlisted keeps its default. The values themselves live in the DTCG file `source` points to.

relationships.description: Links from this theme to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' the tokens it overrides, 'extends' a base theme. Tools derive the reverse edges.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this theme, in order. Themes accept the general kinds only (guidelines, use-cases, accessibility, content, sections, checklist). Tools SHOULD keep this order for display.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Docs for agents (AI/LLM) only, using the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Put anything a human needs in `documentBlocks` — that's the default. Use this only for things that would be noise to a person: hard must/must-not rules, notes that correct a mistake agents commonly make (like picking a similar but wrong entity), evidence-backed constraints, and machine-checkable criteria. A rule humans need too still belongs in `documentBlocks`. This adds to the human docs, it never replaces them. Tools MUST NOT show these blocks to humans. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human docs first.

token.description: One design token: identifier, type, use cases, guidelines, accessibility notes. `name` is optional — left out, the `identifier` is the display label. Description and status are also optional. When a token is inside a group and skips its own status, it inherits the group's.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a token.

identifier.description: The token identifier. No fixed pattern, to support DTCG and design-tool naming styles (dots, slashes, dashes). Also serves as the display label.

name.description: Optional display name (ex: 'Primary Text'). Left out, the `identifier` is the label — the same rule every entity follows.

tokenType.description: The token's type, per the DTCG spec (ex: 'color', 'dimension', 'fontFamily', 'fontWeight', 'duration', 'cubicBezier', 'number', 'shadow'). MUST be set here unless a parent group already declares it — a token inherits the group's value if it skips its own. Every token needs a `tokenType` somewhere in its ancestry; having none anywhere is a defect.

description.description: What this token represents and when to use it. Optional, so keep it terse at scale. CommonMark supported.

source.description: Points to the DTCG (W3C Design Tokens) file. DSDS doesn't copy token values — the DTCG file is the source of truth for resolved values, aliases, and type.

file.description: URI or relative path to the DTCG file containing this token's definition.

path.description: The dot-path to this token within the DTCG file (ex: 'color.text.primary').

metadata.description: Optional metadata: the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json).

relationships.description: Links to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' a token this one aliases, 'part-of' a token group. Tools derive the reverse edges.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this token, in order. Tokens accept the general kinds only. Tools SHOULD keep this order for display.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Docs for agents (AI/LLM) only, using the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Put anything a human needs in `documentBlocks` — that's the default. Use this only for things that would be noise to a person: hard must/must-not rules, notes that correct a mistake agents commonly make (like picking a similar but wrong entity), evidence-backed constraints, and machine-checkable criteria. A rule humans need too still belongs in `documentBlocks`. This adds to the human docs, it never replaces them. Tools MUST NOT show these blocks to humans. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human docs first.

tokenGroup.description: A group of related tokens — a full collection, a family (all color tokens), a sub-family (one hue with its scale), or any other grouping. Groups nest to form a hierarchy: a top-level 'color' group might hold 'color-text', 'color-background', and 'color-border', each with its own tokens. `name` is optional — left out, the `identifier` is the label. Description and status are also optional. A child that skips its own status inherits the group's.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a token-group.

identifier.description: The token group identifier. No fixed pattern, to support DTCG and design-tool naming styles (dots, slashes, dashes). Also serves as the display label.

name.description: Optional display name (ex: 'Primary Text'). Left out, the `identifier` is the label — the same rule every entity follows.

description.description: What this token group covers and how it is organized. Optional, so keep it terse at scale. CommonMark supported.

tokenType.description: If every token in this group shares a type, declare it here instead of repeating it on each child. A child MAY override it. Common values: 'color', 'dimension', 'fontFamily', 'fontWeight', 'duration', 'cubicBezier', 'number', 'shadow'.

source.description: Points to the DTCG (W3C Design Tokens) file. DSDS doesn't copy token values — the DTCG file is the source of truth for resolved values, aliases, and type.

file.description: URI or relative path to the DTCG file containing this group's definition.

path.description: The dot-path to this group within the DTCG file (ex: 'color.text').

children.description: The tokens and/or nested groups inside this group, in order. Order often shows a real progression (lightest to darkest, smallest to largest), so tools SHOULD keep it. Children inherit `tokenType` and status from this group when they skip their own.

metadata.description: Optional metadata: the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json).

relationships.description: Links to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' a token this one aliases, 'part-of' a token group. Tools derive the reverse edges.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this group, in order — the general kinds only. Use this for docs about the group as a whole; put docs for individual tokens on the token itself, inside `children`.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Docs for agents (AI/LLM) only, using the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Put anything a human needs in `documentBlocks` — that's the default. Use this only for things that would be noise to a person: hard must/must-not rules, notes that correct a mistake agents commonly make (like picking a similar but wrong entity), evidence-backed constraints, and machine-checkable criteria. A rule humans need too still belongs in `documentBlocks`. This adds to the human docs, it never replaces them. Tools MUST NOT show these blocks to humans. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human docs first.

anyEntity.description: Every entity has a `kind`. It says which type the entity is, and the `if`/`then` branches below pick the matching schema for it.

fileRef.description: A link to a DSDS entity or entity group in another file. A tool like `json-schema-ref-parser` resolves it before validation and bundling. Point at a specific value with a JSON Pointer fragment (ex: './button.dsds.json#/entity', './groups/tokens.dsds.json#/entityGroups/0').

$ref.description: A relative path to another DSDS file, optionally with a JSON Pointer fragment for one value in it (ex: './button.dsds.json#/entity'). MUST be relative — no absolute paths, protocol-relative paths, or scheme URIs (http:, file:, …). To reference another system, use `extends.url` instead. Resolvers MUST catch cycles, MUST treat a broken file, pointer, or shape mismatch as a fatal error, and MUST only fetch remote files from an allow-list (see the spec's security rules).

entityGroup.description: A named collection of entities. They all live in one `entities` array, in display order, and can mix any type — each entity's `kind` says what it is, so there's no need for separate arrays per type. A group MUST hold at least one entity.

name.description: Human-readable name of the collection (ex: 'Acme Design System', 'Color Tokens', 'Button Documentation'). Used only as a display label.

entities.description: The group's entities, in display order — any mix of kinds. Each item can be inline or a `$ref` to another DSDS file. Order matters; tools SHOULD keep it.
